regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: THE PASSING OF LYMAN ABBOTT

regions.label: text
regions.text:
ON October 21st Dr. Lyman Abbott, former editor-in-chief of the Outlook and successor of Henry Ward Beecher as pastor of the Plymouth Congregational Church, Brooklyn, died in the latter city in his eighty-seventh year. He has not been in the limelight during the past ten years, but in the years between 1890 and 1905 his name was a household word in America.

He was a striking representative of an important phase of American thought. In the late eighties the Darwinian theories of evolution, biblical criticism, the new psychology and the discoveries in astronomy and geology showing that the universe stretched further out in space and reached further back in time than the churchman had been taught to believe created a sensation. Then pious and religious souls began anxiously to inquire whether the new science, psychology and knowledge of the Bible would undermine the faith of the fathers and the eternal foundations of religion.

Then champions of the eternal verities leaped to the fore. Rudolf Lotze, Prof Paulsen, Rudolf Eucken and Prof Otto of Germany, Dr James Martineau, the Seth brothers and the Cairds of England, Dr Wm. T Harris, U S Commissioner of Education, Prof John Watson of Canada, Dean C. C. Everett, Prof William James and Prof Josiah Royce of Harvard, Dr George Trumbull Ladd of Yale, Prof Borden P. Bourne of Boston University and President Jacob Gould Schurman of Cornell University and Prof John Fiske filled the libraries with books that were penetrating, analytical and profound and eloquent in passages showing that modern science had not undermined faith in God, freedom and immortality.

But these works were for the most part too subtle and profound for the undergraduate and for the layman who only had a high school education or a grammar school education supplemented by general reading. Then a group of brilliant preachers came along who acted as the interpreters of the thoughts of the master minds. Dr George A Gordon and Dr C C. Dole of Boston, Dr Theodore Munger and Dr Newman Smythe of New Haven, Coun., Dr Amory Bradford of Montclair and Dr Lyman Abbott of Brooklyn, N. Y., shone brilliantly in this galaxy who distilled the thought of the great scholars and philosophers and gave it to their hearers or the general public in homeopathic doses.

Among this group Dr Lyman Abbott was the most conspicuous. He occupied a strategic position, as he was the successor of the famous Henry Ward Beecher and edited the most popular religious weekly. Then his clear and vigorous style and his faith and enthusiasm, which manifested themselves both in speech and writing, gave him a wide hearing. We remember how the main auditorium and the anterooms of Dwight Hall, Yale University, were packed and jammed one Sunday evening to hear Dr Abbott discourse eloquently for one hour on evolution and religion. Not a great philosopher himself, Dr Abbott was a remarkable popularizer of semi-philosophical thought.

Just judge men not from the light that we right that they had, remembering that they, to their environment and were influenced by the freed with his great predecessor, Henry Ward, was not as magnetic a preacher, but he was intellectual things he was a more philosophy that concern the plain, common, every day, a psychologist as the great Brooklyn preacher of his tunes, the problem of the theological demands of modern thought, Dr Abbott al-

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Dr Abbott was also intensely interested in Negro education but while a progressive in religion, he was a reactionary on the race problem. While a radical in theology, he was a conservative when handling the strivings of the black man. He believed in the industrial, but not the higher education of the Negro, and did not think that the time was ripe for the Negro to press his claims to give and political recognition.

We do not think that this was due so much to prejudice per se as to lack of information about the Negro. In a talk with him while he was college pastor at Harvard for a few weeks we found him frank, courteous and cordial, but he had not known intimately Negro men of real scholarship like Dr Alexander Crummell and Dr. Edward Wilmot Blyden, nor had he read of the distinguished foreign Negroes of the eighteenth century. He did not seem to know of the civilizations that black men erected in Egypt, Ethiopia and Timbucoo. Possibly he had heard of these things, but had not taken them seriously or investigated them for himself, and that is perhaps why a semi-philosophical thinker in religion and theological thought, the great divine was only a surface thinker in ethnological thought.

But we must not criticize Dr Abbott too severely. He came upon the scene of action and arrived at manhood's state long before the researches of Sergi, Ripley, Boaz, Emot, Chamberlain, Werner and Major Felix Du Bois revealed the part played by black men in American, medieval and ancient civilization, in an age when Gregoire's Enquiry was not taken seriously in America and in an age when the American translation omitted the splendid tribute to the Negro in Volney's Ruins and the Meditation on the Fate of Ancient Empires.

We must judge men not from the light that we have, but from the light that they had, remembering that they, too, were the product of their environment and were influenced by the Zeitgeist.

Compared with his great predecessor, Henry Ward Becher, Dr Abbott was not as magnetic a preacher, but he was a greater writer. In intellectual things he was a more philosophical thinker, but in things that concern the plain, common, every day man he was not as keen a psychologist as the great Brooklyn preacher, but in the main problem of his times, the problem of theological readjustment to the demands of modern thought, Dr Abbott ably filled his niche.

regions.label: doc_title
regions.text: LATEST FOREIGN NEWS

regions.label: text
regions.text:
By J. M. B. STEVENS
American Correspondence School of Law and Procedure Highly Congratulated for Graduating Bellasian Genius
Dr. Edward Edgar Eldred Metzen, eldest son of Karl Alexander Metzen, ex-auditor general of the colony of British Honduras, has again been graduated by the wide-world known American Correspondence School of Law of Chicago, Illinois, of the United States of America, after completing very successfully his three-year course in American Law and Procedure. Dr Edward Edgar Eldred Metzen, who carries the titles of Doctor of Law, Master of Arts, Certified Audit Accountant and Trade and Commerce Engineer, is termed the Bellasian genius for his "God-gifted" qualities. He took up the study of American law and procedure in 1818, after craying many long years for it, and to show what a determined genius he is he never rested content until today no one can get his reasons. Dr. Edward Edgar Eldred Metzen entered the Saint John's Birohmans College of the city of Bellas, thereby to all the other schools in that city, and came out a successful scholar. The only answer that he gives to his associates and others who come in contact with this genius when asked his reasons for going in for the American law and procedure in "Let sleeping dog lay; I will be all services to you all yet, my boys, study the bread of international affairs

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: THE CHICAGO TRIBUNE SEEKS BLOOD

regions.label: text
regions.text:
THE Chicago Tribune is evidently doing its very best to precipitate another race riot in the Windy City. Its blood lust was not satisfied by the riot of the summer of 1919. Not enough black men and white men were killed and wounded to satisfy its desire to see blood flow like milk and water. So the Chicago Tribune by a subtle propaganda is endeavoring to stir the blood and arouse the warlike passions of its Caucasian readers.

Recently it had a misleading cartoon, "The Rising Tide of Color," which we commented upon in the columns of The Negro World. Now again in the October 28th issue of the paper it has a misleading editorial entitled "Advice to Negro Leaders," which we quoted last week.

This editorial in the Chicago Tribune

regions.label: text
regions.text:
ly interested in Negro education, he was a reactionary on the raceology, he was a conservative when he man. He believed in the influence of the Negro, and did not think Negro to press his claims to power.

was due so much to prejudice per se at the Negro. He did not seem to ask men erected in Egypt, Ethiopia, I heard of these things, but had agitated them for himself, and that local thinker in religion and theology was only a surface thinker in the Abbott too severely. regions.label: text
regions.text:
and you will very quickly go in for it"

Dr. Edward Edgar Eldred Metzgen is still in the service of His Britannic Majesty, which it is understood he will give up some time later or sooner to carry on his professions in the republica, where he is needed as a minister of the laws of man. He has served with the British government twenty-two years and some months and is giving up even his little life pension, which he says will be of no use to him. The only very solid conclusion that we can come to is that the "God-gifted" genius, who is gifted for foreseeing things, is that there will be some very great change around the United States of America, the West Indies, Central and South America. This is traced to the quotations of Judge Daniel F. Cohsan's speech at the Washington Day dinner in February, 1821. It is claimed by sound authority that this genius is some kind of man to tackle in the problems of life versed in the psychology of mankind. The question arises daily who speaks to him, who sees him passing anywhere, who sees him standing up, is it possible for such a man living? The youngster is needed outside of this possession.

The entire staff and members of the American, Correspondence School of Law and Procedure of the City of Chicago in the State of Illinois in the United States of America is highly congratulated for graduating the "God-gifted" genius. This entire school was at no time aware of the fact that they

regions.label: text
regions.text: had the pleasure of graduating this genius for it is not every institute or school can train him. There is one important point that must not be ignored under any circumstances, and that is. The American Correspondence School of Law and Procedure has won fame. They have made a genuine name in that little colony of British Honduras which no other institute has done, and it is hoped that they will ever keep that name for the system of educating, service, courtesy on the part of the entire staff and members, and encouragements to advance in life. It is impossible to criticise in any way manner or form this noble institute of law, and all that is hoped for is that they may have the pleasure of graduating at least 200 more so that they can fill the foreign calls like our learned Dr Metzgen May the blessings of Almighty God ever be with that institute and guide them always on the road of success, that they ever and ever increase abundantly in anything they may handle in life.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: An August Born

regions.label: text
regions.text:
It is a well known fact that astrological scientists are very keenly interested in the young doctor who was born in the month of August, 1887, between the hours of midnight and 1 o'clock in the morning, when some of the most active and sedate panes were in unison, when no other planet was in the way of disturbances. It is simply marvellous and it would pay any astrologer to come in contact with this genius Dr Metzgen.

Of that which is known of him by very narrow nothing in daily life he is kind-hearted, generous and exceptionally powerful magnetic emotional and possessed of very strong intuitions, which he has never failed to follow from infancy and very strong individually high noble ideals and a most industrious power for good, which inspires others to do good. An exceptional layer of animals flowers, and noted for humane feelings toward the trouble. Godly ordained for high positions of trust and responsibility that of which he is filling all along life's line. As to drawing people of all ages to him in a mystery that cannot be solved. He is a genuine model for a young man who wish to advance in the world, a model for silence of enemies. Nothing is ever known to cause his passions, known never to have to have the feelings of anyone not to win the feelings of the most kind. A youngster of most vivid brain education. His skills can build in sweet command features of gravel brown which can then power it one his majestic powers are felt. His qualities which are shown like the rays of the sun in his looks, expressions of the sun in his speech that shill and captivate her heart, which is one of the golden keys to inspire others to good. He is possessed of the skill of a builder of minions, the art of the engineer, the eye of the artist and the golden eye of an artist. Lower of laws, indeed. An able counselor.

His Love for Children

There is one other point that should always be remembered with the genius Dr Metzgen. He is an exceptional layer of children and has made them also a special study in his laws, for he is totally against cruelty to children putting young boys and girls in prison. He was from his existence with them that it does no good to the poor in fortune children of the world. He is utterly against cruelty to children by the parents some of whom are known to mistreat their children with sticks, non-attention in sickness, non education of children. He is in fact utterly an untending children in darkness of life and believes that the temples must come by all means which will result in having true and most desirable men and women of the different State. He is utterly against the destruction of the young and that all of the man kingdom and of the animal kingdom shall have their breath of Godly air liberty good feelings given them by God the unseen until death takes place. The world ever refuses looking upon such a minister as this young genius Dr Metzgen? No certainly not. He is willing to sit on the corner of the young at any moment called for under these circumstances of the children present, those becoming mothers before time, those being ill-treated, those who are being shaved to the darkness of a world instead of being shown the golden rays of the sunlight. "Father, Father, Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do." It is held very strongly that in time to come the young doctor has declared the States and governments will successfully obliterate all these life of the young unfortunate children with ease and entire success in years to come. The young doctor has made it a study up to now to study fully the nature of mankind, their build, their minds, what they are and what they are not. It will be very interesting to meet the young genius, for it will assist those who know not of what humans are. He is well beloved by all children. Indeed, and his services are of very great help to them whom called upon at any time.

As a Sportman

Dr Metzgen loves sports such as football playing, cricketting, tennis, bathing in swimming pools and his piano music. He indulges in no kind of card games, horse races and such the likes. He is fond of roaming forests, and loves to see the movies. Gardening. But in all these give him his law books.

Dr Metzgen is the Descendant from a very high European family in Copenhagen, Denmark, and it is claimed that those people whom he is the descendant of are some brain people. His grandfather, Karl Metzgen, some years ago was consul for Denmark in Belfise, British Honduras.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
May Leave British Honduras at Any ' Gall

It is learned up to now that the young genius, Dr. Metzgan, may leave British Honduras at any moment's call to fill the field of business, for he has decided that there is room vacant that cannot be filled for the field of law. He is prepared to give full satisfaction whatsoever he is called to. All success and prosperity is wished for

regions.label: doc_title
regions.text: ANNOUNCEMENT EXTRAORDINARY

regions.label: text
regions.text:
the young law-man, and well-earned golden crown bestowed on him.

Subjects Tackled in His Law Examinations Entire Success

First Year—Elementary law, Blackstone's Commentaries, I. Blackstone's Commentaries, II. constitutional law, I. constitutional law, II., personal rights, domestic relations, torts, contracta, partnership, agency, bailments and common carriers, negotiable instruments, principals and aurety

Second Year—Wills and settlement of estates, wills No. 2, sales, equity jurisprudence, private corporations, public corporations, real property, common law pleadings and practice, criminal law, criminal procedure and evidence, international law

Third Year—Legal instruments, abstracting cases, court process, the constitution, statutory law, commercial law, practical thesis work, final examination

All the above subject was tackled by the young genius who have never left a stone unturned in digging the hundreds of volumes by different authorities which today has made him the man of the hour

Service with British Government

During the twenty-two years and some months' service with the British Honduras Government, Dr Metzgen has served in the following departments Treasury Department, Customs Department, Education Department, the King's bonded warehouses, the Attorney-General's Department, the Bellize Prison Department, and the Audit and District Court, sat on commission with committee investigating into the establishment of an industrial school for boys and girls with the keeper of prisons, William Henry Hoar, and many other slight services in other departments. One thing that is looked upon, that all the heads of the departments especially look upon this young man as a hard-working and efficient officer has always borne high recommendations to the different governors he worked under. His experience naturally is broad and ripe, therefore he is compelled by his supreme powers to leave for the fields of operation

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Visited United States In 1909

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Visited United States in 1909
Dr Metzgen visited the United States of America in the year 1909, and has made esteemed friends with hundreds of people. He was in New Orleans, Mobile, Birmingham, Montgomery and the colored institute of Tuskegee, where he met Dr Booker T. Washington and other gentlemen from the West Indies and other parts of the State, who are wishing daily for his return Mr Metzgen gave a very nice account of the treatment meted out to him by the Americans in general, and claimed them to be perfect Christian gentlemen. He in no way was shown contempt, in fact, nor hurt in any way. He was well attended to in all walks of life as a perfect gentleman until he returned to British Honduras.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: His Next Visit Will Be Soon

regions.label: text
regions.text:
His Next Visit Will Be Soon
Dr Metzger's visit to his law school
will take place soon. It is understood
and he will go by way of New Orleans
New York and Chicago.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Centenary Celebration

regions.label: text
regions.text: The celebration of the battle of St. Georges Caye was an entire success on the 11th and 12th of September, 1922. The city was well decorated. Cycle parader motor cars fire engines sociales and the many thousands of school children parading the principal streets of British Honduras in the celebration a mark of history The Americans showed high appreciation with the community. The most attractive feature of the day was the school children, with the American and English flags waving as they passed by, with the strains of the band blending with the voices of the children "Vamos Compadre from St. Georges Caye".

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: REMAINS OF COL. CHARLES YOUNG, U. S. A., TO BE RETURNED TO THE U. S.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
The Colonel Charles Young Post No. 338, American Legion, of New York, has received advices from the War Department relative to the return of the body of the late Colonel, for whom the post has been named.

Colonel Young was military attache to the United States Consul at Monrovia, Liberia. While on a tour of inspection he succumbed to an illness and died at Lagos, Nigeria, last January. Lagos being about 1,500 miles from Monrovia and an English territory, the body, having been buried, was required, according to English rule, to remain six months.

The attached letter received by the post from the War Department will be of interest to the public which has evinced a great interest in the return of this great soldier

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: October 24. 1922.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
To Mr Wm Service Bell, lst Vice Commander, Col Charles Young Post 398, American Legion, 2350 Seventh Avenue, New York, N. Y.
1 Referring to previous correspondence relative to the return to the United States of the remains of the late Colonel Charles Young, you are advised that this office is in receipt of a cable from the American Minister at Monrovia, Liberia, to the effect that authority has been secured of the Nigerian Government for the exhumation of the remains of Colonel Young, also that the Government of Nigeria has consented to take the responsibility of placing the remains on board, and that he is making necessary arrangements with the steamship line at Lagos for transportation of the remains. Inasmuch as the necessary structures have been given relative to the procurement of the casket and details arranged with the Niger Government, it is believed that it be possible to get the remains into country within a very few months.
2 You will be advised fully of the progress made as information is received in this office.
For the Quartermaster General.
H. J. Conner, Assistant.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text:
CASE AGAINST HON. MARCUS
GARVEY SET FOR NOV. 27

regions.label: text
regions.text: The case of the United States vs. Marcus Garvey will be heard in New York on the 27th instant. This case promises to be one of the most interesting in the history of reform movements and their leaders. The world knows that it is not the question of Marcus Garvey, but the ideals of the Universal Negro Improvement Association and the Negro peoples of the world that will be on trial. Negroes everywhere shall organize themselves the more to carry the fight for an emancipated race and a redeemed Africa to the final conclusion. Negro traitors and rival organizations are mainly responsible for the action brought against Mr. Garvey. Mr Garvey, it is stated, will place on the witness stand over a hundred of the plotters who have tried to harass him and the Universal Negro Improvement Association to testify as touching the part in the international conspiracy to incriminate and defend him in the prosecution of the high ideals of Negro Freedom

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: PUBLIC FORUM

regions.label: text
regions.text: There will be a public forum at the library every Thursday evening at 8:30 p.m. The first meeting is Thursday, Nov. 9. Speaker, Mr. George Lloyd Subject, "The Distinction of Privilege Democracy's Greatest Need." Discussion welcomed
